metadata.summary: Split source files at 300 lines or ~10k tokens into 30–200 line single-responsibility units. Improves LLM context fit and human review. Use `git mv` to preserve history; run tests between moves.

content.title: Code Decomposition Principles
content.summary: LLMs produce partial or incorrect diffs when a file plus its tests exceed the effective context window (~20–50k tokens). Humans review poorly past ~300 lines. Modular architectures correlate with 973× higher deployment frequency (2024 DORA report). The DORA findings and Addy Osmani's LLM workflow both confirm that small, single-responsibility files unlock both human and agent productivity.

why.p: LLMs produce partial or incorrect diffs when a file plus its tests exceed the effective context window (~20–50k tokens). Humans review poorly past ~300 lines.

Modular architectures correlate with 973× higher deployment frequency (2024 DORA report). The DORA findings and Addy Osmani's LLM workflow both confirm that small, single-responsibility files unlock both human and agent productivity.

list.item: A target file exceeds ~300 lines and a subagent must edit it under a tight context budget.
LLM keeps producing partial/incorrect diffs because it cannot hold the full file plus tests in one window.
Designing a new module from a spec and laying out the file tree from the start.
Onboarding an LLM to an unfamiliar repo: split first, then have the agent read the new tree.
File is under 100 lines and tightly cohesive — splitting adds indirection without context savings.
Hot path with a measured performance cost from indirection (rare in app code).
Generated code (migrations, protobuf stubs) — apply rules to the source, not the output.
Throwaway scripts and one-shot notebooks where the file is the unit of thought.

section.name: decomposition-principles-and-size-rules
section.p: Concrete file-size thresholds, token-budget planning, and the when-to-split checklist for LLM-friendly code structure.

rule.statement: Each file must have one clear purpose; if you cannot name the file without using "and" or "utils", split it.
rule.rationale: Multi-purpose files grow indefinitely; single-purpose files have a natural size ceiling.

example.title: Layered decomposition
example.code: users/ models.py # Data structure only serializers.py # API representation views.py # HTTP handlers permissions.py # Access control services.py # Business logic tests/ test_models.py test_views.py test_services.py
example.note: Django users module split by layer: models.py, serializers.py, views.py, permissions.py, services.py — each under 200 lines.

rule.statement: Split any file that exceeds its type's max-lines threshold; target the ideal range to start.
rule.rationale: Files beyond ~300 lines require excessive scrolling for humans and exceed effective LLM context when combined with tests.

list.item: Model/Entity: ideal 50-100 lines, max 200 — split by domain
Service: ideal 100-200 lines, max 300 — extract sub-services
View/Controller: ideal 50-150 lines, max 250 — extract handlers
React Component: ideal 50-150 lines, max 250 — extract sub-components
Test file: ideal 100-300 lines, max 500 — split by scenario
Config: ideal 50-100 lines, max 150 — split by environment

section.name: token-budget-planning

rule.statement: If a single source file exceeds 500 lines (~15k tokens), split it before scheduling LLM-assisted edits.
rule.rationale: Effective LLM context is far smaller than nominal; a 500-line file plus its tests and task context saturates a 50k-token window.

section.p: 100k-token budget allocation: task context ~5k, existing code to read ~20k, generation ~30k, testing ~20k, buffer ~25k.

list.item: Lines > 300
Multiple classes or components in one file
Multiple distinct responsibilities
Frequent merge conflicts (multiple people editing)
Hard to test (need to mock too much)
Hard to name (contains "utils", "helpers", "misc")
Circular imports present
LLM produces incomplete or wrong diffs for the file
File is under 100 lines and tightly cohesive
Parts always change together
Splitting would only add indirection without context savings
Would break encapsulation by exposing internals

section.name: decomposition-anti-patterns
section.p: Four common failure modes when splitting code, with examples and corrective rules.

antipattern.description: A single file (often named utils.py or helpers.ts) accumulating 1000+ lines of unrelated functions from across the domain.
antipattern.reason: No single developer owns it; it becomes a dumping ground; every feature touches it causing conflicts.

rule.statement: Split by domain: string_utils.py, date_utils.py, currency_utils.py — not one catch-all utils file.
rule.rationale: Domain-split utilities have clear ownership and a natural scope that prevents further growth.

antipattern.description: One function per file: add.py, subtract.py, multiply.py — each 3 lines.
antipattern.reason: Navigation overhead exceeds the cognitive saving; import chains become deep and fragile.

rule.statement: Set a minimum file size of 30–50 lines; group related operations into a single module.
rule.rationale: Files below 30 lines add import overhead without context savings; group by semantic cohesion.

antipattern.description: A service class receives an HTTP Request object and accesses request.query_params directly.
antipattern.reason: The service is now coupled to the HTTP framework; testing requires a real or mocked request, not plain data.

example.note: Service takes a plain user_id: int — framework-agnostic, testable with a simple integer.

antipattern.description: An index.ts that re-exports everything with export * from './UserService', './OrderService', etc.
antipattern.reason: Wildcard re-exports break tree-shaking; bundlers cannot remove unused exports, inflating bundle size.

example.code: export { UserService } from './UserService'; export type { User } from './types';
example.note: Explicit named exports only; tree-shaker can eliminate unused symbols.

rule.statement: Forbid export * in agent prompts; require explicit named exports.
rule.rationale: Agents generate barrel re-exports by default; the constraint must be in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Mechanical line-count splits that ignore semantic cohesion: user_part1.py / user_part2.py.
antipattern.reason: Arbitrary splits break logical units; require a responsibility-based target tree before any move.

antipattern.description: Agent uses cat old > new && rm old instead of git mv.
antipattern.reason: git blame and git log --follow break permanently; mandate git mv in the system prompt.

antipattern.description: Agent edits only the moved file and forgets to update call sites in sibling packages.
antipattern.reason: Cross-file refactors silently break imports; require grep-first + import-update + typecheck before committing.

section.name: decomposition-workflow
section.p: Analyze-plan-execute-verify loop for splitting oversize files, with planner/executor subagent split and prompt patterns.

rule.statement: Stop the agent after the planning step and require explicit human approval before executing any moves; a wrong target tree cascades into many bad commits.
rule.rationale: Planning errors compound: 50 executor commits based on a flawed tree are far harder to revert than one rejected plan.

list.item: Analyze: identify file size (lines, tokens), list responsibilities, map dependencies, find natural boundaries.
Plan: draw target structure, define interfaces between new modules, order moves so tests stay green after each step.
Execute: create new files with empty structure, move code piece by piece, update imports, run tests after each move, commit each logical step.
Verify: all tests pass, no circular imports, each file has single responsibility, sizes within limits, naming is clear.
Planner (Opus): reads oversize file + direct importers, emits target tree (file → responsibility → est. lines) and move-order respecting tests.
Executor (Sonnet): performs each Extract-Service / Extract-Component / Extract-Module step; runs test suite between commits; uses git mv.
Reviewer (Sonnet): checks SRP, file size, naming after each step; runs madge --circular / pydeps --show-cycles.

rule.statement: Pin a minimum file size of 30–50 LOC in every executor prompt to prevent micro-files.
rule.rationale: Agents over-decompose by default; the minimum enforces meaningful units.

rule.statement: Always pair a decomposition with a green test suite; if tests do not exist, write characterization tests before moving code.
rule.rationale: Without tests, any move can silently change behavior; characterization tests capture current behavior as a safety net.

rule.statement: Track churn x complexity (CodeScene-style); refactor only the top 5–10 hotspots first, not the entire repo.
rule.rationale: DORA research shows high-churn high-complexity files disproportionately drive incidents; targeted decomposition yields the highest ROI.

rule.statement: Use tokei or scc to fast count lines of code per file; identify oversize files quickly before planning a decomposition.
rule.rationale: Line-count tools give the first signal for when a split is needed; they run in milliseconds and have no false positives.

rule.statement: Use radon (Python) or lizard (multi-lang) to find high-complexity functions that should be candidates for extraction even if the file is under the line threshold.
rule.rationale: Cyclomatic complexity correlates with bug density; high-complexity functions are refactoring candidates even at modest file sizes.

rule.statement: Use madge (JavaScript/TypeScript) or pydeps (Python) to detect circular imports after splitting; circular imports are a sign of improper boundaries.
rule.rationale: Circular imports indicate that the module boundaries are not clean; they must be resolved before the decomposition is complete.

rule.statement: Use tiktoken to estimate token count for large files; split any file that exceeds 500 lines (~15k tokens) before LLM edits.
rule.rationale: Effective LLM context is smaller than nominal; splitting large files first prevents context saturation during refactoring.

rule.statement: SonarQube / SonarCloud / CodeScene provide "hotspots" (high-churn × high-complexity intersection); prioritize decomposing top hotspots over systematic splitting.
rule.rationale: DORA research shows that high-churn high-complexity files disproportionately drive incidents; targeting them yields the highest ROI.

template.purpose: Lists oversize files, high-complexity functions, and high-churn hotspots for the planner agent.
Agent prompt: produce target file tree + ordered move list as JSON.
